pattern: Two points already share an activation chain whenever a sequence of shared rows/columns links them, so the existing points split into independent chain-groups; one new point can bridge at most two such groups (one through its row, one through its column) plus itself.
hints: Think of every existing point as sitting on two "lines": its row (fixed y) and its column (fixed x). Activation spreads along a line, so points reachable from one another through any sequence of shared rows/columns form one group.
Compute the size of every such group first (a union-find keyed on row-id and column-id works well, counting how many points end up per merged root).

A brand-new point can only ever touch one row and one column, so it can connect at most two existing groups (plus itself). The best you can do is add it between the two largest groups -- unless there's only one group, in which case just attach to it.

edge_inputs.description: single point, adding one new point always activates exactly two
two points sharing only an x-coordinate
two points sharing only a y-coordinate
two points sharing neither coordinate -- two singleton groups, the new point bridges both
several isolated points, all distinct rows and columns
one large already-connected group plus one distant isolated point -- must pick the isolated one as the second group
coordinates at the extreme allowed magnitude, negative and positive
many points all sharing one column (one big group) -- adding a point elsewhere on that column only extends it by one, not a merge

stress.description: large set of random distinct points across a wide coordinate range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    pts = []
    seen = set()
    while len(pts) < n:
        x = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
        y = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
        if (x, y) not in seen:
            seen.add((x, y))
            pts.append([x, y])
    return [pts]

stress.description: clustered points along a small number of rows/columns, forcing large groups
